The Harenveld Institute, established in 1994 and operating from a dedicated 12,000-square-meter facility at 4 Karrenberg Plein, received a 9.1-million-euro allocation from the Flemish Advanced Research Directorate to pursue device integration studies through 2028.

paragraph: Scientists from the Kelmore Research Institute in Albrecht Bay, Canada, described on March 3, 2025, a nitrogen-doped graphene aerogel with a specific capacitance of 412 farads per gram at a current density of 1 ampere per gram in a 6-molar potassium hydroxide electrolyte, offering a new benchmark for aqueous supercapacitor electrode performance. paragraph: Researchers at the Aveldorn Institute for Nano-Energy Conversion in Tresheim, Austria, announced on April 4, 2025, the fabrication of a perovskite solar cell integrating a reduced graphene-oxide electron transport layer that achieves a certified power conversion efficiency of 26.8 percent under standard AM1.5G illumination at 100 milliwatts per square centimeter. Dr. Lukas Weidenthaler led the research team of ten, who reported a certified open-circuit voltage of 1.21 volts and a fill factor of 86.2 percent. The Aveldorn Institute, located at Tresheimer Strasse 31 in Tresheim, received 7.5 million euros from the Austrian Photovoltaics Advancement Fund to build a 1-square-meter module prototype by 2027.

paragraph: A group at the Varinkala Nanomaterials Laboratory in Jyvaskala, Finland, reported on October 28, 2024, the creation of a single-walled carbon nanotube fiber bundle with a tensile strength of 9.6 gigapascals and a specific modulus of 480 gigapascal-centimeters-cubed per gram, fabricated by a wet-spinning process from a superacid solution containing nanotubes at a concentration of 8 percent by weight. paragraph: The Straventhal Institute of 2D Crystal Growth in Metzlingen, Switzerland, disclosed on February 18, 2025, the synthesis of a new transition metal dichalcogenide, hafnium ditelluride monolayer designated HfTe2-M, that transitions between charge-density-wave and superconducting phases at a critical temperature of 4.7 kelvin under zero applied pressure. paragraph: Scientists at the Drothenveld Institute of Bionanomaterials in Kellenbruch, Germany, announced on September 9, 2024, the development of a boron-nitride quantum dot formulation designated BNQD-5, whose photoluminescence emission at 420 nanometers under 365-nanometer excitation achieves a quantum yield of 91 percent without any heavy-metal sensitizers. paragraph: An international team coordinated by the Grantholm Institute of Nanomaterials Engineering in Hordborg, Sweden, reported on May 5, 2025, the synthesis of a covalent triazine framework designated CTF-88 whose iodine-vapor adsorption capacity reaches 7.4 grams of iodine per gram of framework at 75 degrees Celsius, exceeding all previously published values for porous organic adsorbents by at least 28 percent. paragraph: Investigators at the Varossa Photonics and Materials Center in Lindamar, Portugal, announced on July 21, 2024, the observation of a room-temperature exciton binding energy of 0.62 electronvolts in a tungsten disulfide monolayer grown on a hexagonal boron-nitride substrate, measured by two-photon photoluminescence excitation spectroscopy using a 1,030-nanometer pulsed laser. paragraph: A team at the Renneval Center for Industrial Nanotechnology in Pontaubert, France, reported on March 26, 2025, the development of a graphene-reinforced aluminium matrix composite designated GRAC-11 with an ultimate tensile strength of 680 megapascals and a Vickers hardness of 218 HV0.5, compared to 310 megapascals and 95 HV0.5 for unreinforced 6061 aluminium alloy processed under identical conditions. paragraph: An international team based at the Orellent Institute for Biomimetic Nanomaterials in Cascadia Prime, Canada, published on February 6, 2025, the synthesis of a spider-silk-mimicking polypeptide functionalized graphene nanosheet hybrid designated SSG-3 that combines the toughness of spider-silk beta-sheet nanocrystals with the mechanical reinforcement of graphene, yielding a composite fiber with a toughness of 1,320 megajoules per cubic meter and an ultimate tensile strength of 5.1 gigapascals. The hybrid fiber, described in Bionanomechanical Materials Science, volume 9, issue 2, was wet-spun from a 6-percent-by-weight dope in hexafluoroisopropanol at a draw ratio of 6 and then post-drawn in a methanol bath at a draw ratio of 1.4, yielding a final diameter of 18 micrometers. Dr. Eloise Chambertin and Dr. Ravi Krishnaswami co-led the ten-person team, who reported that SSG-3 fiber resists fracture even after 24-hour immersion in simulated seawater at 35 degrees Celsius. The Orellent Institute, at 1 Cascadia Prime Boulevard, received 6.2 million Canadian dollars from the Canadian Advanced Structural Biopolymers Initiative to produce 1-kilogram quantities for ballistic protection panel testing by 2027.

paragraph: Researchers at the Drenval Centre for Two-Dimensional Polymers in Lochaber, Scotland, described on August 19, 2024, the first synthesis of a fully conjugated 2D polythiophene network designated DTP-2D, grown on a gold surface by on-surface Ullmann coupling of a custom hexabromothiophene monomer at 180 degrees Celsius, producing a honeycomb lattice with a lattice constant of 2.1 nanometers verified by scanning tunneling microscopy at 4 kelvin. The polymer, reported in On-Surface 2D Polymer Chemistry, volume 8, shows a calculated band gap of 1.42 electronvolts from density functional theory and an experimentally measured surface photovoltage of 0.88 electronvolts under 500-nanometer illumination. Dr. Fiona MacCailleach and six collaborators showed that DTP-2D domains extend to 150-nanometer grain size, the largest for any on-surface-synthesized 2D polymer reported to date. The Drenval Centre, located at Lochaber Science Estate, Inverness Road, Building 9, received 3.1 million pounds from the Scottish Advanced Materials Innovation Fund to lift the polymer from gold onto silicon substrates for photovoltaic device testing by 2026.

paragraph: A team at the Vorenthal Institute for Nanomaterial Toxicology in Rijnhaven, the Netherlands, published on May 20, 2025, a comprehensive toxicological assessment of 12 commercial graphene-based nanomaterials demonstrating that epithelial cells from human airway organoids designated HAO-7 show dose-dependent cytotoxicity only above 80 micrograms per milliliter for oxidized graphene nanoplatelets but no measurable toxicity at concentrations up to 200 micrograms per milliliter for edge-hydroxylated graphene produced by the Rijnhaven liquid-phase exfoliation protocol at pH 10. The findings, detailed in Nanomaterial Safety and Regulatory Science, volume 15, issue 5, used a battery of 22 assays including mitochondrial membrane potential, reactive oxygen species generation, and comet assay genotoxicity testing over 72-hour exposures. Dr. Maartje van Schijndel led the 11-person team, which also found that particle lateral size below 500 nanometers is the dominant predictor of cellular uptake, independent of oxygen functionalization. The Vorenthal Institute, at Rijnhaven Harbour Research District, Quay 3, received 6.9 million euros from the European Nanomaterial Risk Governance Council to extend the assessment to in vivo inhalation models and develop a graphene safety classification framework by 2028.
